1: Theme. The words of Agur son of Jakeh — an unknown sage, possibly from the tribe of Massa (north Arabian). The wisdom tradition in Proverbs is explicitly international — Egyptian (Proverbs 22:17-24:22), and here possibly Arabian. Christ as the Wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:24; Colossians 2:3) is the fulfillment toward which all wisdom traditions grope, regardless of national origin. Paul on the Areopagus (Acts 17:27) argues that all nations, in their searching, are seeking the God who is not far from each one of us — Agur's wisdom-searching is a type of this universal groping toward the one who can be found.
2: Theme. Surely I am too stupid to be a man; I have not the understanding of a man — Agur's confessional self-deprecation. The disciples' repeated failures to understand (Mark 9:32: they did not understand the saying, and were afraid to ask him) are the NT form of this honest incomprehension. But Paul extends it further: the natural person does not accept the things of the Spirit of God, for they are folly to him, and he is not able to understand them because they are spiritually discerned (1 Corinthians 2:14). The confession of inability that Agur models is the first movement of the wisdom the Spirit gives.
3: Theme. I have not learned wisdom, nor have I knowledge of the Holy One — the acknowledged gap that sets up the questions of verse 4. Paul: for now I know in part; then I shall know fully, even as I have been fully known (1 Corinthians 13:12). The partial knowledge Agur acknowledges is the condition of all pre-resurrection wisdom. Christ is the one who gives the knowledge of the Holy One: no one knows the Father except the Son and anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him (Matthew 11:27). The gap Agur confesses is precisely what the incarnation closes.
4: Direct. Who has ascended to heaven and come down? Who has gathered the wind in his fists? What is his name, and what is his son's name? Surely you know! — the cosmological questions culminating in the question about the divine Son's name. Christ answers directly: no one has ascended into heaven except he who descended from heaven, the Son of Man (John 3:13). The name Agur could not supply — the Son's name — is given in the annunciation: you shall call his name Jesus (Matthew 1:21). The gathering of wind (Job 38:31-33; Psalms 104:4) and the wrapping of waters are the creative acts Colossians attributes to Christ (Colossians 1:16-17: all things were created through him and for him... and in him all things hold together). Agur's unanswerable question is the OT's most direct anticipation of the Father-Son revelation.
5: Direct. Every word of God proves true; he is a shield to those who take refuge in him — the proven-true divine word as protective shield. Christ: heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away (Matthew 24:35). His words are the proved-true, refined-silver speech of the perfectly reliable God. John 17:17: your word is truth — Christ's prayer is that his disciples be sanctified in the truth of this reliable word. And he himself is the Word (John 1:1) — the proved-true word is not merely a propositional claim but a person in whom the reliability of God is incarnated and demonstrated.
6: Theme. Do not add to his words, lest he rebuke you and you be found a liar — the warning against supplementing the divine word. Revelation closes with the same warning (Revelation 22:18-19) — the canonical boundary is preserved at the beginning and end of the biblical era. Christ fulfilled the law and the prophets (Matthew 5:17) rather than supplementing them — the completion he brought was not addition but fulfillment. The tradition that made void the word of God (Mark 7:13) is the specific form of addition Christ condemned — human supplements that displaced rather than fulfilled.
7: Theme. Two things I ask of you; deny them not to me before I die — the death-aware petition that frames the request of verses 8-9. Christ's Gethsemane prayer is the ultimate pre-death petition: nevertheless, not my will but yours be done (Luke 22:42). Agur's two requests are addressed to God with the explicit awareness of mortality; Christ's prayer is the absolute surrender of the one who faces death in full awareness of its reality. The petitioner who prays before I die anticipates the one whose death becomes the foundation of all subsequent petition.
8: Direct. Remove far from me falsehood and lying; give me neither poverty nor riches; feed me with the food that is needful for me — the petition for the exactly-right portion. The Lord's Prayer: give us this day our daily bread (Matthew 6:11). Agur's lechem chukki (food of my appointed portion) is the precise OT equivalent of the epiousios (daily/appointed) bread of the Lord's Prayer. Both prayers request exactly what is needed for today — neither accumulation nor deprivation — from the Father who knows what his children need before they ask (Matthew 6:8). The Lord's Prayer is the Christological form of Agur's petition.
9: Theme. Lest I be full and deny you... or lest I be poor and steal and profane the name of my God — the twin dangers of abundance and scarcity for covenant faithfulness. The Deuteronomic warning (Deuteronomy 8:10-14) is applied here with acute precision: fullness produces forgetfulness; deprivation produces the desperate act that desecrates the Name. Christ's instruction on anxiety (Matthew 6:25-34) addresses both dangers: the one who trusts the Father neither gorges in abundance nor steals from desperation, because the Father's provision is secured for those who seek his kingdom. The Name is not profaned by those who live from the Father's hand.
10: Revelation of God. Do not slander a servant to his master, lest he curse you and you be held guilty — the moral weight of the unjustly accused servant's curse. Christ as the servant unjustly accused (Isaiah 53:3-4: despised and rejected... we esteemed him stricken, smitten by God, and afflicted, but he was pierced for our transgressions). The false accusations brought against him at his trial carried moral weight — those who brought them were held guilty in the precise sense this proverb describes. Peter's Pentecost sermon: you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men (Acts 2:23) — the slander held its accusers guilty.
11: Theme. There are those who curse their fathers and do not bless their mothers — fifth commandment violation as the seed of all the moral failures in the catalogue (verses 11-14). Christ's summary of the law with the neighbor-love command (Matthew 22:34-40) is grounded in the vertical relationship with God — the one who does not honor the Father cannot keep any of the other commands. The specific parent-cursing failure is a sign of a disordered relationship with authority itself — which is why Christ saw the Pharisees' Corban rule (Mark 7:9-13) not merely as a legal technicality but as the symptom of a heart that had displaced God.
12: Revelation of God. There are those who are pure in their own eyes but are not washed of their filth — the self-assessed purity that is actually defilement. Christ's diagnosis: unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God (John 3:5). The washing that removes actual filth is not self-generated; it is the washing of regeneration (Titus 3:5). The Pharisees who were pure in their own eyes are the clearest NT expression of this proverb — the whitewashed tombs (Matthew 23:27) clean on the outside, defiled within. The only adequate washing is Christ's atoning blood and the Spirit's renewal.
13: Theme. There are those — how lofty are their eyes, how high their eyelids lift! — embodied pride. Christ's observation about the Pharisees who loved the place of honor at feasts and the chief seats in the synagogues (Matthew 23:6) is the social form of the lofty eye. The beatitude that counters it: blessed are the poor in spirit (Matthew 5:3) — the lowered eyes of genuine poverty-of-spirit is the posture of the kingdom. The lofty eye before God (Proverbs 6:17) is the abomination that the cross definitively addresses: the cross is the place where all human loftiness is stripped away and the truth of human need is exposed.
14: Theme. There are those whose teeth are swords... to devour the poor from off the earth — predatory consumption of the poor. The rich man who feasted sumptuously while Lazarus lay at his gate (Luke 16:19-21) is the devouring of the poor that this proverb indicts. The reversal in the parable (Luke 16:25) is Proverbs 30:14's verdict enacted: those who consumed the poor find themselves in want while the devoured Lazarus is comforted. Isaiah's indictment of those who grind the faces of the poor (Isaiah 3:15) and Amos 8:4 (you trample on the needy and bring the poor of the land to an end) are the prophetic developments of the same observation.
15: Theme. The leech has two daughters: Give, Give. Three things are never satisfied; four never say, Enough — insatiability as a category. The spiritual hunger that drives human restlessness is addressed by Christ: everyone who drinks of this water will be thirsty again, but whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again (John 4:13-14). The never-satisfied categories Agur identifies — Sheol, the barren womb, the thirsty earth, fire — find their satisfaction-resolution only in eschatology (Revelation 21:4: death shall be no more) and in the living water that Christ gives as the foretaste of that final satisfaction.
16: Theme. Sheol, the barren womb, the land never satisfied with water, and the fire that never says Enough — the four insatiable things: death, infertility, drought, fire. Christ's resurrection addresses the first directly: death is swallowed up in victory (1 Corinthians 15:54). The barren womb made fruitful is a recurring resurrection-motif (Elizabeth, Luke 1:36). The living water Christ gives satisfies the parched earth of the soul (John 7:38-39). The fire that is satisfied — the Spirit as fire at Pentecost (Acts 2:3) brings the holy consuming fire that purifies without demanding more. Each insatiable thing finds its resolution in Christ's redemptive work.
17: Theme. The eye that mocks a father and scorns to obey a mother will be picked out by ravens of the valley — filial contempt's graphic consequence. The soldiers who mocked Christ at the cross (Matthew 27:27-31) — the contemptuous eye directed at the Father's beloved Son — is the NT form of this parental-dishonoring. The ravens picking out the eye is the unburied-corpse image of the ultimate shame. But the resurrection reverses the shame: the one whose eyes were mocked became the one before whom every eye shall see (Revelation 1:7) — every eye will see him, even those who pierced him.
18: Theme. Three things are too wonderful for me; four I do not understand: the way of an eagle, a serpent on a rock, a ship at sea, and the way of a man with a virgin — the four pathways that leave no trace. The mysteries of movement through their media without residue. The incarnation is the greatest wonder of all: the eternal Word taking a way through the virgin's womb (Matthew 1:20-23; Luke 1:35) — the way of a man with a virgin being the form of the miracle that Agur could only observe as wonder. Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son (Isaiah 7:14; Matthew 1:23) is the divine entering through the path Agur identified as unfathomable.
19: Theme. The way of an eagle in the heavens — the wonder of purposeful movement whose path is invisible. Isaiah 40:31: they who wait for the LORD shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings like eagles. The ascension of Christ (Acts 1:9-11: as they were looking on, he was lifted up, and a cloud took him out of their sight) is the ultimate eagle-way — the ascent through the heavens that left no visible path but opened the way for the Spirit to descend. The disciples were to wait in Jerusalem for what the invisible path had made possible (Acts 1:4).
20: Revelation of God. This is the way of an adulteress: she eats and wipes her mouth and says, I have done no wrong — the self-exonerated moral evasion. The religious leaders who declared if we had lived in the days of our fathers, we would not have taken part with them in shedding the prophets' blood (Matthew 23:30) are the adulteress of this verse — wiping their mouths and declaring innocence while continuing the same pattern. Christ's response: you witness against yourselves (Matthew 23:31). The self-exoneration that Proverbs identifies as the adulteress's character is the defining mark of those who reject Christ while claiming to honor God.
21: Revelation of God. Under three things the earth trembles; under four it cannot bear up — the cosmic-weight of social disorder. Matthew 27:51: at the death of the righteous one, the earth shook (kai he ge eseisthe), tombs were opened. The earth-trembling that Proverbs assigns to social inversions finds its maximum expression at the crucifixion — the one moment when all four of Agur's inversions are simultaneously in play: the wicked are ruling, justice is inverted, the blameless is being destroyed. The earth cannot bear it and shakes. The resurrection is the restoration of order that the earth's trembling demanded.
22: Theme. A servant when he reigns, and a fool when he is filled with food — two of the earth-trembling social inversions. The disciples' dispute about greatness (Luke 22:24-27) brings Jesus's definitive teaching: the kings of the Gentiles exercise lordship over them... but not so with you. Rather, let the greatest among you become as the youngest, and the leader as one who serves. The servant who reigns without having been formed for it is the disaster Proverbs describes; the servant who is given authority through the formation of service (as Christ modeled) is the kingdom's pattern of leadership.
23: Theme. An unloved woman when she gets a husband, and a maidservant when she displaces her mistress — domestic inversions with cosmic weight. The Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55) is the theological response to both: he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate; he has filled the hungry with good things and the rich he has sent away empty. Mary's song is the announcement that in Christ, the earth-trembling inversions Agur catalogues are themselves about to be inverted — the low are exalted, the proud are scattered. The order is restored not by preventing the inversions but by Christ's own redeeming work.
24: Theme. Four things on earth are small, but they are exceedingly wise — wisdom exceeding size. Christ's parables consistently feature the small-as-paradigm: the mustard seed that becomes a great tree (Matthew 13:31-32), the leaven hidden in three measures of flour (Matthew 13:33), the lost coin (Luke 15:8-10). The kingdom of God is the small-and-wise thing that operates by principles opposite to worldly power-measurement. God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong (1 Corinthians 1:27) — the cross itself is the ultimate small-and-wise thing that confounds the wisdom of the world.
25: Theme. The ants are a people not strong, yet they provide their food in the summer — cooperative provision despite weakness. The early Jerusalem church (Acts 2:44-45; Proverbs 4:32-35) embodied the ant-wisdom: all who believed were together and had all things in common... there was not a needy person among them. The community without worldly strength providing for all its members is the social form of ant-wisdom. Christ's feeding of the multitude multiplied the five loaves and two fish — the small provision of the weak, entrusted to Christ, became more than sufficient.
26: Theme. The rock badgers are a people not mighty, yet they make their homes in the cliffs — finding security through knowledge of environment rather than through strength. Peter's confession (Matthew 16:18: you are the Christ, the Son of the living God... and on this rock I will build my church) is the cliffs-for-the-weak made concrete: the weakness of the disciples' position is secured not by their strength but by the Rock — Christ himself. The rock badger who cannot fight but makes its home in inaccessible cliffs is the disciple community that has no worldly power but is established on the indestructible foundation.
27: Theme. The locusts have no king, yet all of them march in rank — self-organizing order without a visible center. The post-resurrection community in Acts operates without a visible human king (Jesus is ascended) yet maintains coherent direction through the Spirit. Acts 4:32: the full number of those who believed were of one heart and soul — the locust-march without a visible king is the Spirit-directed community that moves with coordinated purpose because its king is present invisibly. The ascension created precisely this condition: the king is gone from sight but the community marches.
28: Theme. The lizard you can take in your hands, yet it is in kings' palaces — access through adaptability. Christ's ministry penetrated every social level: he ate with tax collectors and sinners (Matthew 9:10-11), healed Jairus's daughter (a synagogue ruler, Mark 5:22), and commended the faith of a Roman centurion (Matthew 8:10). The gospel itself is the lizard that entered kings' palaces — Paul before Felix, Festus, and Agrippa (Acts 24-26); the gospel reaching Caesar's household (Philippians 4:22). The small, apparently vulnerable thing that cannot be kept out of the highest spaces is the gospel itself.
29: Theme. Three things are stately in their tread; four are stately in their stride: the lion, the rooster, the he-goat, and a king whose army is with him — stately movement as the mark of legitimate authority. Christ's triumphal entry (Matthew 21:5, citing Zechariah 9:9: behold, your king is coming to you, humble and mounted on a donkey) is the stately stride reframed. He enters with the composed dignity of the lion-king — not with military display but with the purposive unhurried movement of one who has authority. Revelation 19:11-16 shows the eschatological form: the rider on the white horse whose armies follow him — the king with his army in their ultimate stately march.
30: Type. The lion, which is mightiest among beasts and does not turn back before any — the lion's fearless authority as the paradigm of legitimate power. Christ is the Lion of the tribe of Judah (Revelation 5:5) who overcomes — the mightiest who does not turn back before any adversary. The paradox of Revelation 5 is that this Lion is also the Lamb who was slain — the fearless advance toward the cross is the lion's stride that did not turn back before death, Hades, or the powers of darkness. He who is in you is greater than he who is in the world (1 John 4:4).
31: Theme. The strutting rooster, the he-goat, and a king whose army is with him — the intermediate and climactic members of the stately-stride catalogue. The he-goat as leader of the flock (Jeremiah 50:8; Zechariah 10:3) is a messianic figure in the prophets — the leader-goat going before the flock. Christ is the shepherd who goes ahead of his sheep (John 10:4: he goes before them, and the sheep follow him, for they know his voice). The king with his army echoes Revelation 19:14 (the armies of heaven, arrayed in fine linen, white and pure, were following him) — the ultimate stately march of the king and his redeemed army.
32: Theme. If you have been foolish, exalting yourself, or if you have been devising evil, put your hand on your mouth — the gesture of self-silencing as the beginning of repentance. Job's response to God's speeches from the whirlwind (Job 40:4: behold, I am of small account; what shall I answer you? I lay my hand on my mouth) is the paradigmatic hand-on-mouth. The disciples' silence in the presence of Christ's glory at the transfiguration (Matthew 17:6: when the disciples heard this, they fell on their faces and were terrified) is the NT form — the appropriate silencing of human self-exaltation before the divine presence. Christ's parable: everyone who exalts himself will be humbled (Luke 14:11).
33: Revelation of God. For pressing milk produces curds, pressing the nose produces blood, and pressing anger produces strife — cause-and-effect at multiple levels. Christ's diagnosis of the heart's cause-and-effect is the deepest form of this principle: out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaks (Matthew 12:34). What is pressed out of the heart determines the outcome — the pressing of the self-controlled heart (which has received Christ's peace, John 14:27) produces peace rather than strife. Paul's instruction: do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good (Romans 12:21) — the pressing of good overcomes the pressing of anger. Agur's final word is wisdom about mechanism — Christ addresses the heart that drives the mechanism.
